title: Pregnancy
summary: Pregnancy changes both influenza risk and medicine choices. Use the fewest medicines needed, check active ingredients, and seek early medical advice for suspected influenza or significant breathing symptoms.
evidenceSnapshot: Paracetamol is generally the preferred medicine for pain or fever in pregnancy when used as directed. Influenza antivirals are most useful when started early, and pregnancy is a recognised higher-risk state.

interventions.name: Rest, fluids, saline spray and warm drinks
interventions.finding: Low-risk measures can ease congestion, throat discomfort and dehydration.
interventions.practical: Use sterile commercial saline or correctly prepared rinse water; drink to thirst.
interventions.cautions: Avoid burns from steam and avoid assuming herbal products are pregnancy-safe.

interventions.name: Paracetamol
interventions.finding: Relieves fever and pain and is generally considered suitable in pregnancy at recommended doses.
interventions.practical: Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest necessary time and check combination products.
interventions.cautions: Seek advice if fever persists or frequent dosing is needed; overdose can seriously injure the liver.

interventions.name: Ibuprofen and other NSAIDs
interventions.finding: They are not routine first-line cold/flu medicines in pregnancy and may be harmful at some stages, particularly later pregnancy.
interventions.practical: Use only if a clinician who knows the pregnancy recommends it.
interventions.cautions: Risks vary by gestation and maternal conditions.

interventions.name: Decongestants
interventions.finding: Systemic decongestants have cardiovascular effects and limited pregnancy safety certainty; saline is preferred.
interventions.practical: Ask a pharmacist, GP or obstetric clinician before oral or medicated nasal decongestants.
interventions.cautions: Extra caution with hypertension, palpitations, placental problems or interacting medicines.

interventions.name: Prescription influenza antivirals
interventions.finding: Early treatment can reduce influenza severity and complications; pregnancy is a high-risk group in whom prompt assessment is important.
interventions.practical: Contact a clinician promptly when influenza is suspected rather than waiting several days.
interventions.cautions: Prescription-only; dosing and choice require clinical assessment.

practicalSteps: Check every ingredient in a cold-and-flu product rather than relying on the brand name.
Tell the pharmacist the gestational week and all medicines and supplements being used.
Test for COVID-19 or influenza when advised, especially with fever, marked aches or exposure.
Keep taking prescribed asthma or other chronic-disease medicines unless the treating clinician advises a change.
avoidOrCare: Avoid multi-ingredient products unless each ingredient is needed and confirmed appropriate.
Do not use herbal remedies, high-dose vitamins or essential oils internally simply because they are 'natural'.
Avoid overheating, dehydration and delaying assessment for breathlessness or reduced fetal movement.

myths.claim: No medicine at all is safer than treating a fever.
myths.reality: Untreated significant fever can also matter; paracetamol is generally preferred when needed, alongside clinical advice.

myths.claim: Natural cold remedies are automatically safe in pregnancy.
myths.reality: Many have limited pregnancy data, variable ingredients and possible interactions.

redFlags: Breathing difficulty, chest pain, fainting, blue lips or severe wheeze
Persistent high fever, inability to keep fluids down or signs of dehydration
Reduced fetal movement, contractions, bleeding or any obstetric concern
Rapid deterioration or suspected influenza in someone with additional risk factors
unsettled: Pregnancy safety data are sparse for many complementary products and some OTC decongestants; absence of evidence is not evidence of safety.
